content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob love playing games, and this time they are playing with an array of NNN integers. They take turns alternatively, with Alice having the first turn. In a player's turn, the player can pick any number (say xxx) which is greater than 1 from the array. Then the player transforms the number xxx into (x×i)/p(x \times i) / p(x×i)/p, where ppp is any prime number which divides xxx and iii is an integer in the range [1,p)[1, p)[1,p), that is 1≤i<p1 \leq i < p1≤i<p. Then the player places the transformed number back in the array in place of xxx. The player who cannot make a move loses.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT, which is the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case denotes the size of the array which is NNN.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers, which represent the original array.


Output Format
Print one line per test case, i.e., the winner of the game in that test case. It should either be "Alice" or "Bob".
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases per file won't exceed 2 * 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, which is the number of test cases. The first line of each test case denotes the size of the array which is N. The second line of each test case contains N space-separated integers, which represent the original array.
content.outputFormat: Print one line per test case, i.e., the winner of the game in that test case. It should either be 'Alice' or 'Bob'.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
In the first test case, the array is originally {2, 2}. Alice has no choice but to pick up one of the 2. And she has no choice in p either. p = 2 is the only choice, as no other prime number divides 2. i = 1 is the only choice. So Alice transforms one of the 2 into (2 * 1) / 2 = 1. So now the array becomes {1, 2} or {2, 1}. Now, Bob has no choice but to pick up the remaining 2. And as with Alice, he will be forced to transform it into 1. The array then becomes {1, 1} and now Alice cannot make any move, and so Bob wins.
In the second test case, the original array is {2}, and as in the previous test case, Alice will convert it into {1} and now Bob has no move, and so Alice wins.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to understand the rules of the game and find a way to determine the winner. The key observation is that the game is a variant of the Nim game, where the players take turns removing objects from a set of piles. In this case, the piles are the numbers in the array, and the players can transform a number into a smaller number by dividing it by a prime factor and multiplying it by a number less than the prime factor. We can use the concept of Nim-sum to determine the winner of the game.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the maximum size of the array. The time complexity is linear because we need to iterate over each number in the array for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the maximum size of the array. The space complexity is linear because we need to store the numbers in the array.
